coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent traceable nanoparticles by co-self-assembly of carbon dot-drug conjugate and biodegradable hyperbranched polymer for diagnosis and therapy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Guoping
Pei, Mingliang
Li, Xinming
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more interest in tumor theranostics, but they suffer from the rapid renal clearance due to small size and high hydrophilicity. To solve such problems, hydrophobic pH-triggered carbon dot-drug conjugate (CDs-Hy-DOX) with high doxorubicin (DOX) content of 48.23% were designed by covalent conjugation of DOX onto the CDs via acid-labile linkage with hydrazine (Hy) as bridge. Then the fluorescent traceable hybrid prodrug nanoparticles were fabricated via co-self-assembly with the CDs-Hy-DOX as pH-sensitive prodrug and a pH/reduction dual-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy as polyethylene glycol (PEG)-based surfactant, as well as gatekeeper for pH/reduction dual-triggered DOX release. The hybrid prodrug nanoparticles with hydrodynamic diameter of 220 nm and DOX content of 22.99% were obtained with the optimized co-self-assembling condition. They could release 68.98% of DOX in the simulated tumor microenvironment within 3 days in a sustained release mode, with a premature drug leakage of 7.58%. After the acid-triggered DOX release from the CDs-Hy-DOX, which was accelerated by the pH/reduction dual-triggered degradation of the hyperbranched polymer, the strong fluorescence of CDs-Hy was recovered, demonstrating the promising potential in future tumor nanotheranostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor nanotheranostics
Carbon dots
Hyperbranched polymer
Tumor-specific
Fluorescent imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles

item-info.ce:copyright: Chinese Society of Particuology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of acid-labile CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug (a), synthesis of reduction-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy (b), and fabrication of the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles and their pH/reduction dual-triggered disintegration (c).
TEM images of CDs (a), self-assembled PEG-PO-Cy (b) and CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy (c) nanoparticles.
FT-IR (a) and UV–vis (b) spectra of the samples.
1H NMR spectrum of PEG-PO-Cy(1:0.9) in DMSO-d6.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CDs (0.01 mg/mL) and (b) CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy (0.01 mg/mL), and (c) CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy (0.2 mg/mL) in pH 5.0 ABS + 10 mM GSH for different times at excitation wavelength λ ex = 370 nm. Inset: digital images of the dispersions under 365 nm UV (left) and daylight (right).

In vitro drug release profiles from the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles in different media (a) and the in vitro cellular toxicity of free DOX, blank PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles and CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles (b). The statistical significance was set at *p < 0.05.

CLSM images of HepG2 cells after incubation with the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles for 48 h. The label a, b, and c represent the images of the channels of DOX, DAPI, and CDs, respectively.
Fitted parameters with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent traceable nanoparticles by co-self-assembly of carbon dot-drug conjugate and biodegradable hyperbranched polymer for diagnosis and therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more interest in tumor theranostics, but they suffer from the rapid renal clearance due to small size and high hydrophilicity. To solve such problems, hydrophobic pH-triggered carbon dot-drug conjugate (CDs-Hy-DOX) with high doxorubicin (DOX) content of 48.23% were designed by covalent conjugation of DOX onto the CDs via acid-labile linkage with hydrazine (Hy) as bridge. Then the fluorescent traceable hybrid prodrug nanoparticles were fabricated via co-self-assembly with the CDs-Hy-DOX as pH-sensitive prodrug and a pH/reduction dual-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy as polyethylene glycol (PEG)-based surfactant, as well as gatekeeper for pH/reduction dual-triggered DOX release. The hybrid prodrug nanoparticles with hydrodynamic diameter of 220 nm and DOX content of 22.99% were obtained with the optimized co-self-assembling condition. They could release 68.98% of DOX in the simulated tumor microenvironment within 3 days in a sustained release mode, with a premature drug leakage of 7.58%. After the acid-triggered DOX release from the CDs-Hy-DOX, which was accelerated by the pH/reduction dual-triggered degradation of the hyperbranched polymer, the strong fluorescence of CDs-Hy was recovered, demonstrating the promising potential in future tumor nanotheranostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotheranostics have been widely investigated for both cancer diagnosis and treatment recently (Thorat & Bauer, 2020). Among the fluorescent inorganic materials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted intense interests (Boakye-Yiadom et al., 2019), owing to their excellent photo-stability, cytocompatibility, and fluorescent property. For example, one of the most widely used chemotherapeutics, doxorubicin (DOX), has been loaded onto the CDs via non-covalent bonds or dynamic covalent bonds as tumor theranostics with declined fluorescence intensity in comparison with the bare CDs (Gong et al., 2016; Soo, Lee, Lee, Bae, & Na, 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2017), because of the impaired optoelectronic properties of CDs by the radical surface interactions, intramolecular rotational motions and other non-radiative decays (Ren et al., 2019) or static quenching (Li, Liu, Wu, Yi, & Zhang, 2018) and dynamic quenching (Gong et al., 2018) mechanisms. After the DOX release in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, the fluorescence of CDs could be recovered for imaging function. Although the satisfactory theranostic performance has been reported, they suffer from the rapid kidney clearance due to their very small diameter (<10 nm) and outstanding water solubility (Yu & Zheng, 2015), restricting their practical application.

Therefore, the CDs-based tumor nanotheranostics were usually designed as composite nanoparticles or nanoassemblies with desired diameter to avoid the kidney clearance. In the designed structures, the CDs were usually modified irreversibly on their surface amino or carboxyl groups (Jia, Pei et al., 2016, 2016b; Pei, Pai, Du, & Liu, 2018; Pei, Jia, & Liu, 2018; Pei, Li, & Liu, 2020; Wang et al., 2017). Especially, the modification from their surface amino groups would cause an obvious decline in the fluorescent intensity (Dong, Du, & Liu, 2020; Iannazzo et al., 2017). Furthermore, the close stacking of the CDs in the composite nanoparticles or nanoassemblies also led to a fluorescence quenching by π−π interactions (Pei et al., 2020). Therefore, for an ideal theranostics, a suitable diameter and very weak fluorescence are desired during the blood circulation, while the disintegration into pristine CDs could occur during the drug release in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, recovering the strong fluorescence for imaging diagnosis.

By now, with a suitable hydrophilic/hydrophobic ratio, various CDs-based nanoassemblies have been designed by the self-assembly of CDs (or CDs-DOX conjugates) with various polymers or liposomes to achieve pH-sensitive DOX release and fluorescence recovery in the recent years (Chen et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2018; Li, Pei, & Liu, 2020; Singh et al., 2017; Xue et al., 2018). However, the pH-sensitive property could only control the intracellular drug release, both the tumor cells and the normal cells, so the differences in glutathione (GSH) or reactive oxygen species (ROS) level between the tumor cells and the normal cells could be used to achieve the tumor selectivity.

As a biocompatible and biodegradable polymer, polyphosphoesters could be degraded via acid-triggered hydrolysis or phosphodiesterase I (PDE I) (Wang, Yuan, Du, Yang, & Wang, 2009), such characteristic could be used as stimuli-responsive drug carriers. To achieve the tumor intracellular disintegration, reduction-cleavable bonds such as disulfide (Ju et al., 2018) or diselenide bond (Ma, Liu, He, Zhang, & Ni, 2018), or ROS-activatable thioketal (Li et al., 2019) or thioether (Wang et al., 2019) bond had been introduced into the skeleton of polyphosphoesters. Furthermore, better structural stability of the self-assembled nanostructures with hyperbranched polymers is expected upon extreme dilution, compared with those of linear polymers, owing to the stronger physical interaction of multiple chain tangles of the hyperbranched structure (Zhang, Shi, Shi, Kaneko, & Chen, 2019).

In the present work, acid-labile CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug with high drug content was designed (Scheme 1 (a)). It showed weak fluorescence in the normal physiological medium. After cell internalization, the conjugate between the CDs and DOX could be cleaved to release DOX and CDs with high fluorescence intensity. Both functions of drug release and fluorescence imaging could be realized simultaneously. To obtain the tumor selectivity and control the diameter of the assemblies of the hydrophobic CDs-Hy-DOX, a pH/reduction dual-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer (PEG-PO-Cy) containing acid-labile phosphoester (P–O–C) and phosphoramide (P–N) groups and bioreducible disulfide bonds was designed as both surfactant and dual-sensitive gatekeeper (Scheme 1(b)). After a facile co-self-assembly process (Scheme 1(c)), the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles were obtained as tumor nanotheranostics for the tumor intracellular pH/reduction dual-triggered DOX release and fluorescence imaging.
The N-doped CDs were prepared via the microwave method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials (Pei et al., 2020).

The hydrazine modified CDs (CDs-Hy) were prepared via the amidation between the CDs and hydrazine hydrate. 0.50 g of CDs, 0.60 g of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) and 0.15 g of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were dissolved into 60 mL of water. After stirring for 2 h at the room temperature to activate the carboxyl groups, 10.0 mL of hydrazine hydrate was added into the solution and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 36 h. The products were separated by dialysis (MW = 500 Da) against water for 3 days by replacing the dialysate every 4 h, and collected via lyophilization, as yellow-brown powder.

50 mg of CDs-Hy were dispersed into 5.0 mL DMSO via ultrasonication. 50 mg of DOX·HCl, 250 μL of triethylamine (TEA) and 25 μL of trifluoroacetic acid were added into the dispersion. The mixture was then stirred in N2 atmosphere at room temperature in dark for 24 h. Finally, the resultant CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug was separated by dialysis (MW = 1000 Da) against pH 7.4 phosphate buffered saline (PBS) for 4 days by replacing the dialysate every 4 h, and collected via lyophilization, as red powder.

Cystamine was prepared by treating cystamine dihydrochloride with NaOH in aqueous solution and abstracted with dichloromethane. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, δ, ppm): δ = 2.55 (4H, 2CH 2S), δ = 1.27 (4H, 2CH 2N), δ = 2.79 (4H, NH2) (Fig. S1, in the Supplementary data).

The pH/reduction dual-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer (PEG-PO-Cy) was synthesized according to the previous report (Sun et al., 2018), with different mass feeding ratios of mPEG/POCl3 (1.0:0.5, 1.0:0.9, and 1.0:1.4). Typically, with the mPEG/POCl3 of 1.0:0.9, 1.0 g of mPEG and 0.55 mL of POCl3 were dissolved into 15.0 mL of dried CHCl3 and the solution was stirred for 2 h at room temperature. Then a CHCl3 solution of cystamine (1.0 g) and TEA (3.45 mL) was added slowly, followed with stirring for 24 h at room temperature. After the reaction, the salts were removed by filtration, and the liquid phase was washed by dilute hydrochloric acid (1 M), saturated NaHCO3 aqueous solution and saturated brine for three times. The organic phase was separated, collected, dried with magnesium sulfate anhydrous and precipitated into anhydrous diethyl ether at 0 °C twice. Finally, the PEG-PO-Cy was obtained as white powder after vacuum drying at 4 °C.

The CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles were fabricated via the co-self-assembly of the CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug and the hyperbranched PEG-PO-Cy, as following: 30.0 mg of CDs-Hy-DOX and 30.0 mg of PEG-PO-Cy were dispersed into 10 mL of DMSO via ultrasonication for 4 h, followed with stirring for 12 h. Then the mixture was dialyzed with pH 7.4 PBS (MW = 14,000 Da) for 3 days, by replacing the dialysate every 6 h, and collected via lyophilization, as red powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The acid-labile CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug was prepared by conjugation of the broad-spectrum anticancer drug, doxorubicin (DOX), onto the CDs-Hy via an acid-labile hydrazone linkage as dynamic covalent bond from the acylhydrazine groups, as well as the acid-labile imine bond from the original amino groups on the CDs (Scheme 1(a)). Due to the static quenching (Li et al., 2018) and dynamic quenching (Gong et al., 2018) mechanisms as well as the π−π interaction (Pei et al., 2020), the CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug exhibited low fluorescent intensity. It is expected that the high fluorescent intensity of CDs-Hy could be recovered after the acid-triggered drug release in the acidic tumor intracellular microenvironment (Dong et al., 2020), in which the amino groups on the CDs-Hy could be released. Furthermore, the positively charged acylhydrazine groups on the CDs-Hy could also be released after the acid-triggered cleavage of the hydrazone conjugation, which make the CDs-Hy well dispersed as single quantum dot in the acidic tumor intracellular media, avoiding the fluorescence quenching by π−π interaction and subsequently favoring the strong fluorescence emission for intracellular imaging application.

The as-prepared CDs showed spherical shape with mean particle size of 2.2 nm in the TEM analysis (Fig. 1 (a)). The mean hydrodynamic diameter (D h) was determined as 3.0 nm with a very narrow distribution in the DLS analysis (Fig. 2 (a)). The CDs-Hy also showed a very narrow diameter distribution around 3.5 nm (Fig. 2(b)). After conjugation of DOX via acid-labile linkages, the resultant products could not be well dispersed into water, due to the hydrophobicity. The strong absorbance peak at 1705 cm–1 of CO in carboxylic acid groups disappeared (Fig. 3 (a)), indicating the complete amidation with hydrazine. Furthermore, the shoulder peak at 1686 cm–1 of the CN bond revealed the successful conjugation of DOX via the acid-labile linkages (hydrazone and imine bonds), as showed in Scheme 1(a). The DOX content in the CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug was measured as high as 48.23% with the UV–vis method, indicating the high drug conjugation efficiency owing to the plentiful surface functional groups, mainly the carboxylic acid and amino groups.

To well disperse the hydrophobic CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug in water with desired diameter and introduce reduction-sensitive targeting function to release the tumor selectivity, a pH/reduction dual-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy was designed as both gatekeeper and surfactant for the acid-labile CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug (Scheme 1(b)). With the PEG-PO-Cy synthesized with a mPEG/POCl3 feeding ratio of 1.0:0.9 as an example, the characteristic absorbance of −CH2 (2885 cm−1), C–O–C (1112 cm−1), P=O (1280 cm−1), P–O–C (1148, 963 and 843 cm–1) and P–N (1242 cm–1) groups could be seen in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S2(a), in the Supplementary data) (Benobeidallah, Benhamida, Kaci, & Lopez-Cuesta, 2020; Gupta & Lopina, 2005; Li et al., 2020). The number-averaged molecular weight was determined as 2.88 × 104 g/mol from the GPC analysis (Fig. S2(b), in the Supplementary data). The results revealed the successful synthesis of the reduction-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy. In the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 4 ), the characteristic signals of the chemical shifts at δ = 3.51 (4H, 2CH 2O), δ = 3.2 (3H, CH 3O), δ = 3.03 (4H, 2CH 2NH), and δ = 2.80 (4H, 2CH 2SS) revealed the successful polymerization of the trifunctional monomer (POCl3) and bifunctional monomer (cystamine) in presence of the monofunctional monomer (mPEG) as end capping agent (Sun et al., 2018). Furthermore, the integral area ratio between the mPEG (δ = 3.2 ppm) and cystamine (δ = 3.03 ppm or δ = 2.80 ppm) was about 2:5, indicating the hyperbranched topology of the designed pH/reduction dual-triggered degradable polymer.

With all three mPEG/POCl3 feeding ratios, the resultant pH/reduction dual-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymers could self-assembled into nanoparticles due to their unique chemical structure with hydrophobic hyperbranched polyphosphoamide core and hydrophilic surface PEG brushes. However, the hydrophilic/hydrophobic segment ratios, which were adjusted by the mPEG/POCl3 feeding ratios, showed an important effect on the diameter of the self-assembled hyperbranched polymer nanoparticles (Fig. 2(c–e)). The well-defined nanoparticles around 186 nm with a very narrow distribution were obtained by the self-assembly of the PEG-PO-Cy synthesized with a mPEG/POCl3 feeding ratio of 1.0:0.9. So, the hyperbranched polymer was used for the fabrication of the pH/reduction dual-triggered prodrug nanoparticles.

After the co-self-assembly of the CD-Hy-DOX as pH-sensitive prodrug and the pH/reduction dual-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy as both gatekeeper and surfactant via the hydrophobic interaction between the hydrophobic CD-Hy-DOX prodrug and the hydrophobic core of the hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy, the pH/reduction dual-triggered CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles were obtained (Scheme 1(c)). They showed a spherical shape with particle size of 80–100 nm from the TEM analysis (Fig. 1(c)), similar as the PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles (Fig. 1(b)). The D h of the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles was 220.7 ± 11.0 nm (Fig. 2(f)). The DOX content was determined as 22.99% by the UV–vis method, near the theoretical value, demonstrating the high co-self-assembly efficiency.

Compared with the UV–vis absorption at 230 nm and 350 nm attributed to the π−π* transitions of the aromatic CC bond in the core and the n−π* transitions of the CO bond and amino groups on the surface of the N-doped carbon dots (Li et al., 2020), the CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug only exhibited the characteristic absorption of DOX (Fig. 3(b)). The disappearance of the absorption at 350 nm was caused by the static quenching (Li et al., 2018) and dynamic quenching (Gong et al., 2018) by conjugation of DOX by the acid-labile hydrazone and imine bonds, as well as the π−π interaction between the aggregated CDs (Pei et al., 2020), leading to a declined fluorescent intensity of the CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug.

The CDs possessed the excitation-independent fluorescence property, with maximum PL intensity at excitation wavelength of 370 nm, with strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV (Fig. 5 (a)). As for the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles, the fluorescent intensity declined obviously (Fig. 5(b), due to the fluorescent quenching by drug conjugation and aggregation.

To evaluate the fluorescence behavior in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles were treated with pH 5.0 ABS + 10 mM GSH. Clearly, the fluorescent intensity increased with prolonging the treating time (Fig. 5(c)). The results demonstrated that the high fluorescence of CDs could be recovered after the acid-triggered drug release. Compared with the original CDs (Fig. 5(a)), the fluorescent intensity could not be completely recovered. It was resulted from the following two reasons: the released CDs-Hy were not the original one but the hydrazine functionalized ones from their surface carboxyl groups, and the dynamic quenching of the hydrazine functionalized CDs (CDs-Hy) with the released DOX via π−π stacking interactions between conjugated carbon skeleton in CDs and benzene rings in DOX (Gong et al., 2018). On the other hand, the CDs-Hy were positively charged, making well dispersed as single quantum dot in the acidic tumor intracellular media, avoiding the fluorescence quenching by π−π interaction (Pei et al., 2020) and subsequently favoring the strong fluorescence emission for intracellular imaging application.

The pH/reduction dual-triggered drug release from the proposed CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles was conducted at 37 °C in pH 7.4 PBS and pH 5.0 ABS with or without GSH, to simulate in vitro the DOX release behavior in the normal physiological medium and the tumor intracellular microenvironment, respectively. No obvious burst release could be seen in the two release profiles (Fig. 6 (a)). There is almost no drug release in the first 9 h in pH 7.4 PBS. The drug release was only 6.51% in 80 h. In the simulated normal physiological medium (pH 7.4 PBS +10 μm GSH), the drug leakage started after 4 h, and the premature drug leakage was only 7.58% in 80 h. Such low premature drug leakage is expected to minimize the toxic and side effects of the chemotherapeutics on the normal cells and tissues during the body circulation.

As in the acidic media (pH 5.0 ABS), the conjugated DOX was released quickly in the first 30 h after an induction period of 2 h, and reached equilibrium in about 60 h, with a cumulative release of 45.63% in 80 h. The results demonstrated that the hyperbranched polymer could not degrade in the pH value. In the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0 ABS + 10 mM GSH), the induction period was shortened to 0.5 h, and the drug release reached 68.98% in 80 h, showing a sustained release behavior. The in vitro drug release demonstrated the excellent pH/reduction dual-triggered drug release performance of the proposed fluorescent traceable hybrid prodrug nanoparticles.

The drug release behaviors in both acidic media were similar in the first 30 h, regardless of GSH. It could be deduced that the drug release in the stage should be caused by the pH-triggered release from CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrugs in the superficial and shallow layers of the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles, due to the hydrophilicity of the hyperbranched polymer (Zhang, Pei, & Liu, 2019). After that, the GSH molecules in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment could diffuse into the resultant looser CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles to cleave the disulfide bonds in the hyperbranched polymer. And the degradation of the hyperbranched polymer resulted into a sustained drug release from the interior CDs-Hy-DOX hybrid prodrug. The drug release reached equilibrium after 80 h, with a cumulative release of about 70%. It should be caused by the non-covalent adsorption of the released DOX molecules with the CDs-Hy and the degraded products of the hyperbranched polymers, which could not diffuse through the dialysis bag with MW = 1000 Da, as reported previously (Li et al., 2020).

The above discussion has been revealed by the hydrodynamic diameter change of the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment. The hydrodynamic diameter increased with prolonging the treating time to 12 h (Fig. 2(g)), meaning the swelling degree of the CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles increased by the gradient drug release and cleavage of the disulfide bonds in the hyperbranched polymer. After 72 h, the species with diameter around 1200 nm appeared, due to the formation of the aggregates of the DOX adsorbed CDs-Hy. Besides, very small species of several nanometers were also produced, which should be the free DOX adsorbed CDs-Hy.

The drug release was simulated with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models (Fig. S3, in the Supplementary data) and the main parameters were summarized in Table 1 . In all the four releasing media, the correlation coefficients (R2) with the Higuchi model were higher than those with the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, indicating the Fickian diffusion controlled release mechanism (Chebil et al., 2019). Furthermore, the kinetic constant (k) increased with increasing the acidity or GSH level in the releasing media, also demonstrating the pH/reduction dual-triggered drug release behavior of the proposed CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles.

The cellular uptake of the proposed CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles was assessed in the HepG2 cells with the CLSM technique after incubation for 48 h. Both the strong red fluorescence of DOX and the strong green fluorescence of the CDs could be seen in the HepG2 cells (Fig. 7 ). After merging the images with that of the nuclei stained with DAPI with blue fluorescence, it is clearly the red fluorescence and the green fluorescence could be overlapped completely with the blue fluorescence (a + b and b + c), respectively. The results indicated that the proposed CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles could be internalized into the HepG2 cells. After the intracellular pH-triggered DOX release, the released DOX (red fluorescence) and the CDs-Hy (green fluorescence) were accumulated into the nuclei (Li et al., 2020; Pei et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2017). Therefore, both the tumor cell growth inhibition and the fluorescence imaging function are expected with the proposed CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles.

The cellular toxicity of the blank PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles and the final CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles on the HepG2 cells was evaluated in vitro with the MTT assays, in comparison with the free DOX. The cell viability was >83% after co-incubation with the blank PEG-PO-Cy nanoparticles for 48 h, even with a high concentration of 20 μg/mL (Fig. 6(b)), demonstrating the excellent cytocompatibility of the reduction-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy as the reduction-sensitive gatekeeper and surfactant for the fluorescent traceable prodrug nanoparticles.

As for the HepG2 cells incubated with the proposed CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles, the cell viability decreased with increasing the dose, showing a dose-dependent growth inhibition similar as the free DOX, although the apparent cellular toxicity of the proposed nanotheranostics seemed lower than the free DOX (Fig. 6(b)). Apparently, the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of the PEG-CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles was 3.21 DOX equivalent μg/mL under such incubation condition, which much lower than that of free DOX (5.37 μg/mL), indicating the enhanced tumor growth inhibition.

In fact, the real DOX concentration was only 2.3 μg/mL from the prodrug nanoparticles (20 μg/mL) after 48 h of incubation, considering the DOX content of 23% and cumulative release of 50% within 48 h. At such a low released DOX concentration, the cell viability was only 44.9%, near to that after co-incubation with the free DOX at 10 μg/mL for 48 h (43.1%). The results demonstrated the enhanced antitumor efficacy of the proposed CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles than the free DOX, due to the P-glycoprotein (P-gp) efflux pump inhibition of the proposed prodrug nanoparticles via endocytosis, rather than the passive diffusion of the free DOX (Pei et al., 2019).

In summary, novel pH/reduction dual-triggered fluorescent traceable prodrug nanoparticles were designed by the facile co-self-assembly of the acid-labile carbon dots-based hybrid prodrug CDs-Hy-DOX with the pH/reduction dual-triggered degradable hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy as both surfactant and gatekeeper. The designed hyperbranched polymer PEG-PO-Cy could not degrade at the acidity of the tumor intracellular microenvironment but could be triggered by the high GSH level. The feature could be used for the tumor-specific drug delivery. The resultant CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles showed weak fluorescence and excellent pH/reduction dual-triggered DOX release. And the strong fluorescence of the CDs-Hy could be recovered after the disintegration of the prodrug nanotheranostics in the tumor intracellular microenvironment, exhibiting promising potential for fluorescent traceable drug release. The released CDs-Hy and DOX were mainly accumulated in the cell nuclei, possessing an enhanced antitumor efficiency than the free DOX. The proposed pH/reduction dual-triggered fluorescent traceable CDs-Hy-DOX@PEG-PO-Cy prodrug nanoparticles are expected as nanotheranostics for tumor treatment.
The authors have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (grant No. 18JR3RA271).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.partic.2021.04.016.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Combined effect of palygorskite and melamine polyphosphate on flame retardancy properties of polyamide 11 nanocomposites
Applied Clay Science
Carbon dots: Applications in bioimaging and theranostics
International Journal of Pharmaceutics
Process conditions for preparing well-defined nano- and microparticles as delivery systems of alkyl gallates
Particuology
Dual-pH sensitive charge-reversal drug delivery system for highly precise and penetrative chemotherapy
Pharmaceutical Research
Carbon dots-cluster-DOX nanocomposites fabricated by a co-self-assembly strategy for tumor-targeted bioimaging and therapy
Particle & Particle Systems Characterization

Journal of Materials Chemistry B
Phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped hollow carbon dot as a nanocarrier for doxorubicin delivery and biological imaging
ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces
